The study of dynamics and control is the study of how change happens. In any system that is subject to change: the way that change progresses (or doesn't) is necessarily subject to the mathematics of stability and chaos. "Attractor" is just math speak for "stable".

reply_context.text: "Allostasis" is not just a property of organisms: it's a property of any sufficiently complex structure that requires energy input to be maintained. The reason I think the "attractor story" is inescapable is because it's a generalized language for discussing the nature of change in any system.

reply_context.text: I'll need to pick up Kate's book, but at least at the surface: "re-synthesizing constraints" sounds to me like it describes a process that is subject to an optimization pressure, so it *has* to be compatible with the attractor story.

posts.text: (or rather, I disagree that "attractor" necessarily means we're buying into *Friston's* FEP. We can have explanatory theories that invoke "Free Energy" style reasoning that do not make all of the claims Friston makes)

reply_context.text: I agree that the FEP research agendas is aligned with the attractor story, but I disagree that the former implies the latter. FEP is stronger and makes (imho unnecessary) claims about pressures towards discovering new information.

reply_context.text: Again, I'd really recommend Kate's book. It critiques free energy principle work, which operates on that assumption of seeking stability in attractor states. She's convincing that this doesn't capture intentional agency. The alternative—resynthesizing enabling constraints—may do better.

posts.text: Instead of calculating loss over uniformly diverse minibatches: throw a different task cluster at each data parallel rank and reduce following the same topology as the task tree. That way, the batch has the same diversity overall, while also encoding an informed prior for the task representation.

reply_context.text:
So we built Banyan: a procedurally-generated environment where you can systematically vary the components that define a task.

A Banyan task is composed of a layout & a task tree, where each tree is defined by its topology and the objects assigned to its nodes.

posts.text: Lifting to the more general notion of attractors, rather than invoking "allostasis" (which privileges organism-scale biological processes) reveals how this framework explains most things. "Viability <--> Attractor" characterizes any process that settles into an evolutionarily stable state.

reply_context.text: Even more broadly: "allostasis" is just a special case of an attractor. A "learned model" is also an attractor: it's a low energy region in the cost landscape associated with some kind of prediction.

posts.text: Even more broadly: "allostasis" is just a special case of an attractor. A "learned model" is also an attractor: it's a low energy region in the cost landscape associated with some kind of prediction.

reply_context.text: So there's no simple decision point or homunculus setting goals. Rather, goal seeking is viability seeking in action, and the brain is seeking viability at a high level of abstraction, receiving signals from the entire body and balancing them, while applying a learned model. That's allostasis.
